summary: Gruber provides a detailed analysis of Apple's September 2008 'Let's Rock' event, covering iTunes 8, new iPods, and NBC's return to the iTunes Store. He notes that while the announcements were solid incremental improvements, the absence of year-over-year growth charts signals iPod market saturation. The new iTunes Genius feature impresses but has metadata-matching flaws. NBC's return after a year-long standoff yielded the network essentially nothing beyond lost revenue. The iPod Classic shrinks in relevance while the Nano and Touch receive meaningful upgrades, with the Touch being positioned as a handheld gaming competitor.
key_points: Apple's omission of iPod growth charts signals market saturation, though iPhone cannibalization partly explains the slowdown
NBC's return to iTunes after a year-long withdrawal gained them almost nothing — no DRM concessions, no variable pricing on new shows — just millions in lost revenue
iTunes 8's Genius recommendation engine works well but suffers from metadata-matching problems with CD-ripped tracks
The iPod Classic's highest capacity dropped from 160GB to 120GB — the first year-over-year capacity decrease — signaling its declining importance
The iPod Touch received major price cuts and hardware additions (speaker, volume controls), positioning it as a gaming competitor to Nintendo and Sony handhelds
money_quotes: Growth can only come where there's room to grow, which is why even Mac sales are growing faster than iPod sales.

It's a win for everyone — Apple, NBC, and customers — that NBC shows are back, but there's nothing NBC has today that they wouldn't have had if they'd never pulled their shows from iTunes a year ago — except for millions of dollars in lost revenue.
To my recollection, this is the first time that the size of the highest-capacity iPod has gone down year over year.
The hard-drive-based iPod Classic continues to fade toward irrelevance.
The recommendation engine seems pretty damn smart.
His tone wasn't so much apologetic as it was scornful. One gets the feeling Steve Jobs was about as happy with the problems in the 2.0 iPhone OS as the rest of us.
key_insight: Apple's 2008 iPod event revealed a maturing product line where the real story wasn't what was announced but what was conspicuously absent — growth numbers — signaling the iPod's peak and the iPhone's ascendance.
